q3: The opening sequence took a few rounds to land — the model kept leading with the news before acknowledging the wait, which felt like it was skipping a beat. It got there eventually, but it took explicit feedback to fix something that should have been intuitive from context.
q5: I did look at it, and honestly it maps pretty closely to what I actually care about — the emotional arc sequencing, reader-centered framing, and the operational closing criteria are all real. q3: The model kept routing the action item through Marcus's team rather than landing it directly on him — 'have your team update' instead of just telling Marcus to do it. It took an explicit callout to fix that. The opening framing also started sender-centered before I pushed back.
q5: I looked at the rubric and it's a pretty accurate map of what I care about — the emotional arc sequencing, reader-centered framing, operational closings. Seeing it named explicitly probably helped the model recover faster after my feedback rather than drifting in the wrong direction again.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model was consistently good at fixing things I flagged but not great at anticipating them unprompted — it needed the rubric plus my feedback together to get there. Without the rubric I might have just thought the model was slow to learn my style, but actually it seems like it needs explicit structure to hold the priorities in the right order.

q3: The 'why it matters' section kept defaulting to abstract or sender-framed reasoning — it took a few rounds to get it grounded in what the change actually costs the reader (re-sequencing mid-sprint). The closing also had a habit of softening after the CTA, which I had to push back on.
q5: Yes, I looked at it. It captured the structure I care about pretty well — the emotional arc sequencing and reader-centered framing especially. I think it helped the model stop re-centering the sender's reasoning in the 'why' section, which was the main sticking point. That said, the rubric alone didn't get the model there immediately — I still had to give explicit feedback to make it click.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice the model was consistently better at structure than at framing — it could get the order right but still slip into generic stakes language that could've been lifted from any scope-change email.
